title: Replace single-label training with a multi-horizon discrete-time stop ladder
summary: Recurrent-event literature and F1 dynamic-programming papers both frame pit timing as a sequential event process, not a one-off binary classification.
rationale: Recurrent-event literature and F1 dynamic-programming papers both frame pit timing as a sequential event process, not a one-off binary classification. A concrete upgrade over a plain next-lap label is to model `PitWithin1`, `PitWithin2`, `PitWithin3`, etc., then derive a consistent next-lap hazard. That usually stabilizes learning near the decision boundary because laps immediately before a stop are highly related.
implementation_hint: From train only, derive auxiliary targets such as stop within 1/2/3 laps until next actual pit, censoring end-of-race laps. Fit shared-feature models per horizon or a multitask booster/stack, then add monotonicity checks so P(within1) <= P(within2) <= P(within3). Feed the horizon outputs back into the final next-lap classifier as OOF features.
expected_effect: Should sharpen the model around imminent-stop windows and capture timing pressure more smoothly than hand-tuned threshold features alone.
risk: Need careful label construction around race end and immediately after pit stops; sloppy horizon labels can inject contradictory supervision.
